verses.text: Joshua was old and stricken in years; the LORD said unto him: Thou art old and stricken in years, and there remaineth yet very much land to be possessed. This is the land that yet remaineth: all the borders of the Philistines, all Geshuri — from Sihor before Egypt unto the borders of Ekron northward, counted to the Canaanite; five lords of the Philistines: the Gazathites, Ashdothites, Eshkalonites, Gittites, Ekronites; also the Avites: from the south, all the land of the Canaanites, Mearah beside the Sidonians, unto Aphek, to the borders of the Amorites; the land of the Giblites, all Lebanon toward the sunrising, from Baal-gad under mount Hermon unto the entering into Hamath. All the inhabitants of the hill country from Lebanon unto Misrephoth-maim, all the Sidonians: them will I drive out from before the children of Israel: only divide thou it by lot unto the Israelites for an inheritance, as I have commanded thee. Now therefore divide this land for an inheritance unto the nine tribes, and the half tribe of Manasseh. Guidance: God states a fact most leaders prefer not to acknowledge: 'thou art old.' Then He gives a task that does not match the energy: 'divide the land that yet remaineth.' Much is unfinished. God assigns the elderly Joshua the work of distributing what he will never personally conquer. He must trust the next generation to take what He himself only divided on paper. Faith at the end of a career is signing deeds for battles others will fight.

With whom the Reubenites and the Gadites have received their inheritance, which Moses gave them, beyond Jordan eastward, even as Moses the servant of the LORD gave them; from Aroer that is upon the bank of the river Arnon, and the city in the midst of the river, and all the plain of Medeba unto Dibon; and all the cities of Sihon king of the Amorites which reigned in Heshbon, unto the border of the children of Ammon; and Gilead, and the border of the Geshurites and Maachathites, and all mount Hermon, and all Bashan unto Salcah; all the kingdom of Og in Bashan, which reigned in Ashtaroth and in Edrei, who remained of the remnant of the giants: for these did Moses smite, and cast them out. Nevertheless the children of Israel expelled not the Geshurites, nor the Maachathites: but the Geshurites and the Maachathites dwell among the Israelites until this day. Guidance: An unfinished line on a finished map. Most of the east is taken; two pockets — Geshur, Maacah — remain unevicted. The narrator includes the failure in the deed itself. Centuries later David will marry a Geshurite princess (2 Sam 3:3) and produce Absalom from her — the unfinished work of one generation becomes the trouble of the next. Compromise is not neutral; it has descendants.

Only unto the tribe of Levi he gave none inheritance; the sacrifices of the LORD God of Israel made by fire are their inheritance, as he said unto them. Guidance: A single-verse interruption with the chapter's deepest theology. The tribe of the priests gets no farm. Their portion is what burns on the altar — and what burns on the altar belongs to God. They do not eat fields; they eat fellowship. Moses said the same line in Deut 18:1. To serve God closely is to depend on God daily; the man with no acreage learns earlier than anyone else what Psalm 16:5 will later sing: 'The LORD is the portion of mine inheritance.'

Moses gave unto the tribe of the children of Reuben inheritance according to their families. Their coast was from Aroer that is on the bank of the river Arnon, and the city that is in the midst of the river, and all the plain by Medeba; Heshbon, and all her cities that are in the plain; Dibon, Bamoth-baal, and Beth-baal-meon, and Jahazah, Kedemoth, Mephaath, Kirjathaim, Sibmah, Zareth-shahar in the mount of the valley, Beth-peor, Ashdoth-pisgah, Beth-jeshimoth, and all the cities of the plain, and all the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites, which reigned in Heshbon, whom Moses smote with the princes of Midian, Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, Reba, dukes of Sihon, dwelling in the country. Balaam also the son of Beor, the soothsayer, did the children of Israel slay with the sword among them that were slain by them. The border of the children of Reuben was Jordan, and the border thereof. This was the inheritance of the children of Reuben after their families, the cities and the villages thereof. Guidance: Reuben — the firstborn who lost the birthright — gets a southeastern parcel. The interesting interjection is Balaam's death: not in his own oracle scene, but in this dry inheritance ledger. The man who tried to curse Israel for hire dies as collateral in a Midianite skirmish. God's accounts close on every name eventually. Even a prophet for hire is bookkeeping in the inheritance roll.

The border of the children of Reuben was Jordan, and the border thereof. This was the inheritance of the children of Reuben after their families, the cities and the villages thereof. Guidance: Reuben — the firstborn who lost the birthright — gets a southeastern parcel. The interesting interjection is Balaam's death: not in his own oracle scene, but in this dry inheritance ledger. The man who tried to curse Israel for hire dies as collateral in a Midianite skirmish. God's accounts close on every name eventually. Even a prophet for hire is bookkeeping in the inheritance roll.

Moses gave inheritance unto the tribe of Gad, even unto the children of Gad according to their families. Their coast was Jazer, and all the cities of Gilead, and half the land of the children of Ammon, unto Aroer that is before Rabbah; from Heshbon unto Ramath-mizpeh, and Betonim; from Mahanaim unto the border of Debir; in the valley, Beth-aram, Beth-nimrah, Succoth, Zaphon, the rest of the kingdom of Sihon king of Heshbon, Jordan and his border, even unto the edge of the sea of Chinnereth on the other side Jordan eastward. This is the inheritance of the children of Gad after their families, the cities, and their villages. Guidance: Gad gets the middle east bank — fertile Gilead, the cities by the Jabbok. Jacob first prayed in Mahanaim and wrestled in Peniel around here. The granddaughter-tribes settle where the grandfather met God in the dark. The map of inheritance is also a map of memory: every parcel has a patriarch's footprint on it.

Moses gave inheritance unto the half tribe of Manasseh: this was the possession of the half tribe of the children of Manasseh by their families. Their coast was from Mahanaim, all Bashan, all the kingdom of Og king of Bashan, all the towns of Jair which are in Bashan, threescore cities. Half Gilead, Ashtaroth, Edrei, cities of the kingdom of Og in Bashan, were pertaining unto the children of Machir the son of Manasseh, even to the one half of the children of Machir by their families. Guidance: The northeast — Bashan, sixty Jair towns, the fortress cities of Og. Manasseh is the only tribe to be split, half east, half west of Jordan. Joseph's elder son lives on both sides of the river — a bridge tribe. Sometimes God plants a family with a foot on each shore so neither side forgets the other.

These are the countries which Moses did distribute for inheritance in the plains of Moab, on the other side Jordan, by Jericho, eastward. But unto the tribe of Levi Moses gave not any inheritance: the LORD God of Israel was their inheritance, as he said unto them. Guidance: Chapter closes the way verse 14 opened: Levi has no map of his own. The other tribes inherit acres; Levi inherits the LORD Himself. The text is careful to repeat the line. Some callings do not yield real estate; they yield direct access. To be without land in this verse is to be without competition for it elsewhere — when your portion is God, you cannot be evicted by any human deed.
